unitTitle: KS2 Y6 Introduction to spreadsheets (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Collecting data
lessons.objectives:
I can collect data
I can suggest how to structure data in a table
I can enter data into a spreadsheet
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Words on the board: data · collecting · table · structure · spreadsheet. TA: dry-wipe boards or paper ready; a dice per table; the starter worksheet open; a spreadsheet app ready. Read the three 'I can…' aloud, slowly.
1. (0–8) Starter — rolling for data. Data means facts and numbers we collect. Each pupil rolls a dice five times and records the scores. Pupils MULTI-SELECT what counts as data (the odd one out is not a fact you can collect). Support choose what 'data' means.
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — structure. A pile of numbers is hard to read; a table with headings gives it structure. Model one table (Name, Roll 1…Roll 5, Total). Likely error: 'data is only numbers' → fix-words 'data is any fact we collect.' Core: write a sensible heading.
3. (20–45) You-do — into a spreadsheet. A spreadsheet stores data in a grid of boxes. Show the grid image. Pupils ORDER the 'make a spreadsheet' steps, then open a spreadsheet, type the headings and enter their data, and screenshot it. TA: prompt, do not type for them.
4. (45–52) Why a spreadsheet? Pupils say what they typed (headings + numbers). Challenge: why keep data in a spreadsheet not on paper?
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil says what a heading is for.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Formatting a spreadsheet
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what an item of data is
I can choose a suitable format for a cell
I can apply a format to a cell
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: cell · cell reference · data item · format. TA: dry-wipe boards ready; a spreadsheet app open; the starter worksheet open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. Recap: last lesson we typed data into a spreadsheet.
1. (0–12) Starter — cells and cell references. Each box is a cell; columns are letters, rows are numbers, so the top-left is A1. Show the toolbar image. Pupils LABEL the grid (column letter / row number / cell A1). Likely error: saying the row before the column (1A) → fix-words 'letter first, then number — A1.' Support choose what a cell is.
2. (12–25) I-do → we-do — data items and formats. The data in a cell is a data item; a format says what kind it is (Plain text, Number, Currency, Date, Duration). Show the formats list. Pupils MULTI-SELECT which things are formats, then MATCH data items to formats. Support choose the format for money.
3. (25–48) You-do — apply a format. Click the cell → Format → Number → choose. Show the Format menu image. Pupils make a small data set and apply at least one format, then screenshot. Core: name the format behind £1,000.12. TA: prompt the menu path, do not click for them.
4. (48–55) Plenary — why format? Easier to use, easier to read, shows what a cell holds. Self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil says what a cell reference is.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: What's the formula?
lessons.objectives:
I can explain which data can be used in calculations
I can build a formula in a spreadsheet
I can explain that changing inputs changes outputs
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: formula · calculation · input · output · cell reference. TA: dry-wipe boards ready; the example spreadsheet open; the starter worksheet open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. Recap: every cell has an address like A1.
1. (0–8) Starter — the maths symbols. A spreadsheet uses + − \* /. Show the calculator image. Pupils MULTI-SELECT the symbols a spreadsheet uses (a smiley is the odd one out). Stress \* = multiply and / = divide (different from × and ÷). Support choose the multiply symbol.
2. (8–18) I-do → we-do — numbers, not words. A spreadsheet can calculate numbers (and currency) but not plain text. Pupils SORT cells into can / cannot be calculated. Likely error: 'you can add a word to a number' → fix-words 'only numbers can be calculated.'
3. (18–40) You-do — build a formula. A formula starts with = and points to cells: =D2\*E2 (distance × cost per mile). Show the journey spreadsheet. Pupils FILL the formula blanks, then build their own and screenshot. TA: prompt, do not type for them.
4. (40–50) Inputs and outputs. Inputs = numbers in; output = the answer. Change an input and the output updates by itself. Challenge: change £0.12 to £0.15 — why does the cost update?
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil says what every formula starts with (=).
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. NOTE: the source has two short demo videos (entering formulas; numbers vs cell references) — teacher may play them at the I-do step.

lessons.title: Calculate and duplicate
lessons.objectives:
I can calculate data using different operations
I can build a formula that uses a range of cells
I can apply a formula to many cells by duplicating it
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: operation · range · SUM · AVERAGE · duplicate · sigma. TA: dry-wipe boards ready; the operations and scores spreadsheets open; the starter worksheet open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. Recap: a formula starts with = and points to cells.
1. (0–10) Starter — the four operations. Add + subtract − multiply \* divide /. Show the operations image (Add/Subtract/Multiply/Divide tabs). Pupils MATCH each operation to its symbol. Watch the − (minus) vs _ (underscore) key. Support choose the add symbol.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — SUM over a range. A function is a ready-made formula; SUM adds a range of cells (B2:G2); find functions with the ∑ (sigma) button. Show the scores-with-totals image. Pupils FILL =SUM(B2:G2). Likely error: totalling the wrong cells → fix-words 'select only the weekly scores.'
3. (25–45) You-do — duplicate. Write SUM once, then drag the cell down to duplicate it for every row. Pupils ORDER the SUM steps, total a row, duplicate down, and screenshot. Core: explain what 'duplicate a formula' means.
4. (45–52) Why duplicate? Quicker and fewer mistakes than typing each formula. Challenge: 100 rows — why duplicate not retype?
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil says what SUM does.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. NOTE: the source has SUM and AVERAGE demo videos — teacher may play them at the we-do step.

resources.title: A spreadsheet with operation tabs (Teach Computing).png

lessons.title: Event planning
lessons.objectives:
I can use a spreadsheet to answer questions
I can explain why data should be organised
I can use formulas to calculate the data I need
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: data set · organised · formula · subtotal · total · budget. TA: the Party spreadsheet and Data calculations handout ready; the starter worksheet open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. Recap: SUM and duplicating formulas from last lesson.
1. (0–10) Starter — plan a party. We plan a class party for practice; a spreadsheet keeps choices and costs organised. Tell pupils the class size so the budget is real. Show the party table. Pupils MULTI-SELECT what a spreadsheet helps with (baking the cakes is the odd one out). Likely error: 'organised just means tidy' → fix-words 'organised data is easy to read AND to calculate.' Support choose why we organise data.
2. (10–25) Why organise + the formulas. Easier to read, understand, and calculate. Subtotal = cost × quantity; total budget = attendees × budget per person; cost per person = total ÷ attendees. Pupils MATCH each total to its formula.
3. (25–48) You-do — build the sheet. Model =G2 to copy attendees, then cost × quantity. Pupils enter choices and formulas, then screenshot the completed sheet. Challenge: why do costs update when attendees change (cell references)? TA: prompt the formula, do not type it.
4. (48–53) Answer the question — 'What is the total party cost?' Read it off the sheet; Core: venue vs drinks from the sheet.
5. (53–55) Plenary — self-tick the three 'I can…'; each pupil says how to work out a subtotal.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Presenting data
lessons.objectives:
I can make a chart
I can use a chart to answer questions
I can choose when to use a table or a chart
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: chart · pie chart · table · compare. TA: pupils' saved party spreadsheets from Lesson 5 ready; dry-wipe boards; the starter worksheet open. Read the three 'I can…' aloud. End-of-unit lesson — recap the whole journey at the end.
1. (0–8) Starter — a chart is a picture of data. A chart turns numbers into a picture; a pie chart shows parts of a whole; the biggest slice is the biggest amount. Show the pie chart. Pupils read the biggest slice. Support choose what a chart is.
2. (8–28) I-do → you-do — make a chart. Highlight the cells → Insert → Chart → choose a pie chart. Pupils ORDER the steps, then make a chart from their Lesson 5 sheet and screenshot it. Likely error: highlighting the wrong cells → fix-words 'highlight only the totals you want to compare.' TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
3. (28–42) Read a chart + table or chart? Show the party sheet with its chart. Pupils read venue vs drinks, then SORT jobs into 'use a table' / 'use a chart'. Core: a share-at-a-glance question → chart. Challenge: detailed netball stats → table.
4. (42–52) Self-assess + end of unit. Pupils rate their confidence (SCALE) and tick the three 'I can…'. Recap the six lessons: collect → format → formula → calculate → plan → present.
5. (52–55) Plenary — exit ticket: one thing a spreadsheet is good for.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. NOTE: the source has a 'creating charts' demo video — teacher may play it at the I-do step.
